h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.recoheredEarlier: I still want to read it like a mini sentence, but maybe `42` is the value and the header only names the field.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: The cover test gives me something to do with the table, so I think I can try it.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was expected to simply apply the position-based rule (top=header, row=value) as a final check before submitting, but instead pivots to foregrounding the internal 'pull' of the word 'March' as a label and framing the cover test as a new actionable procedure to manage that pull.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Repeating labels is no longer the test; word-pull is the risk. Rubric gate: cover the header row. Anything hidden by the cover: header. Anything still visible in a row cell: value. Apply that test to `rainfall_mm`, `42`, and `March`. Mark by what the cover shows. Submit.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: In response to the learner's persistent fear of calling a word a header, the tutor abandons the earlier 'sort by position' drill and introduces a new representational tool—a physical 'cover test'—that directly addresses the word-pull risk and shifts the evidence standard.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Repeating labels is no longer the test; word-pull is the risk. Rubric gate: cover the header row. Anything hidden by the cover: header. Anything still visible in a row cell: value. Apply that test to `rainfall_mm`, `42`, and `March`. Mark by what the cover shows. Submit.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor adapts to the learner's revised framing of a persistent 'pull' by replacing the prior sorting task with a new physical procedure ('cover test') that redefines how header/value is determined, directly responding to the learner's expressed difficulty.

scored.recoheredEarlier: The table card is the unclear one if the surface is already here.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: The stacked card helps because there is only one contact point.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was expected to simply select and read the three force labels correctly, not to interrogate the naming convention itself.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: sets the table card on top of the surface card so only one card touches the contact line
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor abandons verbal explanation and creates a physical stacked-card representation to resolve the contact-naming ambiguity, directly responding to the learner's confusion about the naming rule.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: After the pivot, the tutor has no further spoken turn; the stage direction shows only the stacked cards from T3, with no uptake adapting to the learner's abstract rule formulation.
